title: The Archaeology of a Breakfast Stall
text_zh: 我家楼下的早餐摊已经开了二十多年了，但我从来不知道老板的名字。我只知道他凌晨三点半就开始和面，四点半第一锅油条下锅，五点钟准时开张。整条街的生物钟是被他的油锅声校准的。他的摊位没有招牌，只有一块被油烟熏得发黄的塑料布遮着头顶。点餐不需要说话——你站在摊前，他看你一眼就知道你要什么。这不是读心术，而是二十年重复观察的结晶。他知道穿西装的中年男人赶时间，要一根油条一杯豆浆带走；他知道背书包的小学生会犹豫很久，最后还是选炸糕；他知道遛弯回来的老太太要一碗热豆腐脑，多放一勺卤。我曾经试过打破这种默契，故意点了一样从来不点的东西。他愣了一秒，然后抬头看了我一眼——那个眼神里没有不满，只有一种类似于「今天你怎么了」的温和困惑。那一刻我意识到，我以为自己只是一个匿名顾客，但在他的认知地图里，我是一个有着固定早餐偏好的具体的人。他的摊位像一个微型的考古遗址——那口用了十几年的铁锅底部结着一层厚厚的碳化物，那是无数次高温油炸留下的地质层。每一层都记录着某个清晨、某个饥肠辘辘的上班族、某个赖床迟到的孩子。如果有一天这个摊位消失了，带走的不只是一种味道，而是整条街二十年的晨间记忆。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā lóu xià de zǎocān tān yǐjīng kāi le èrshí duō nián le, dàn wǒ cónglái bù zhīdào lǎobǎn de míngzi. Wǒ zhǐ zhīdào tā língchén sān diǎn bàn jiù kāishǐ huó miàn, sì diǎn bàn dì yī guō yóutiáo xià guō, wǔ diǎn zhōng zhǔnshí kāizhāng. Zhěng tiáo jiē de shēngwùzhōng shì bèi tā de yóu guō shēng jiàozhǔn de. Tā de tānwèi méiyǒu zhāopai, zhǐyǒu yī kuài bèi yóuyān xūn de fā huáng de sùliào bù zhē zhe tóudǐng. Diǎn cān bù xūyào shuōhuà——nǐ zhàn zài tān qián, tā kàn nǐ yī yǎn jiù zhīdào nǐ yào shénme. Zhè bú shì dúxīnshù, ér shì èrshí nián chóngfù guānchá de jiéjīng. Tā zhīdào chuān xīzhuāng de zhōngnián nánrén gǎn shíjiān, yào yī gēn yóutiáo yī bēi dòujiāng dài zǒu; tā zhīdào bēi shūbāo de xiǎoxuéshēng huì yóuyù hěn jiǔ, zuìhòu háishi xuǎn zhà gāo; tā zhīdào liú wān huílái de lǎo tàitai yào yī wǎn rè dòufunǎo, duō fàng yī sháo lǔ. Wǒ céngjīng shì guò dǎpò zhè zhǒng mòqì, gùyì diǎn le yī yàng cónglái bú diǎn de dōngxi. Tā lèng le yī miǎo, ránhòu tái tóu kàn le wǒ yī yǎn——nàge yǎnshén lǐ méiyǒu bùmǎn, zhǐyǒu yī zhǒng lèisì yú「jīntiān nǐ zěnme le」de wēnhé kùnhuò. Nà yī kè wǒ yìshí dào, wǒ yǐwéi zìjǐ zhǐ shì yī gè nìmíng gùkè, dàn zài tā de rènzhī dìtú lǐ, wǒ shì yī gè yǒu zhe gùdìng zǎocān piānhào de jùtǐ de rén. Tā de tānwèi xiàng yī gè wēixíng de kǎogǔ yízhǐ——nà kǒu yòng le shí jǐ nián de tiěguō dǐbù jié zhe yī céng hòu hòu de tànhuàwù, nà shì wúshù cì gāowēn yóuzhá liú xià de dìzhì céng. Měi yī céng dōu jìlù zhe mǒu gè qīngchén, mǒu gè jīchánglùlù de shàngbānzú, mǒu gè lài chuáng chídào de háizi. Rúguǒ yǒu yī tiān zhège tānwèi xiāoshī le, dài zǒu de bù zhǐ shì yī zhǒng wèidào, ér shì zhěng tiáo jiē èrshí nián de chénjiān jìyì.
text_en: The breakfast stall downstairs from my home has been open for over twenty years, but I've never known the owner's name. I only know he starts kneading dough at three-thirty in the morning, the first batch of fried dough sticks goes into the oil at four-thirty, and he opens at five o'clock sharp. The entire street's biological clock is calibrated by the sound of his frying. His stall has no sign, just a sheet of plastic tarp yellowed by cooking smoke overhead. Ordering requires no words—you stand at the stall, he glances at you and knows what you want. This isn't mind-reading but the crystallization of twenty years of repeated observation. He knows the middle-aged man in a suit is in a hurry and wants a fried dough stick and a cup of soy milk to go; he knows the elementary student with a backpack will hesitate for a long time and still choose fried cake; he knows the elderly woman returning from her morning walk wants a hot bowl of tofu pudding with an extra spoonful of savory sauce. I once tried to break this unspoken understanding and deliberately ordered something I'd never ordered. He froze for a second, then looked up at me—his gaze held no displeasure, only a gentle puzzlement akin to 'what's with you today?' In that moment I realized that though I thought I was just an anonymous customer, in his cognitive map I was a specific person with fixed breakfast preferences. His stall is like a miniature archaeological site—the iron wok he's used for over a decade has a thick layer of carbonized residue at the bottom, geological strata left by countless episodes of high-temperature frying. Each layer records some dawn, some hungry commuter, some child who overslept and arrived late. If this stall were to disappear one day, it would take with it not just a flavor, but twenty years of the entire street's morning memory.

questions.q_zh: 老板是如何知道每个顾客想要什么的？
questions.q_en: How does the owner know what each customer wants?

questions.q_zh: 作者把早餐摊比作什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author compare the breakfast stall to?

options.text: 一座博物馆
options.pinyin: yī zuò bówùguǎn
options.text_en: A museum

options.text: 一个微型的考古遗址
options.pinyin: yī gè wēixíng de kǎogǔ yízhǐ

options.text_en: A bustling market

title: The Liturgy of Making Dumplings
text_zh: 在我的记忆里，包饺子从来不是烹饪，而是一场家庭仪式。每年除夕下午，外婆会在厨房的大桌上铺上一层薄薄的面粉，然后把一大团发好的面放在中央——那个动作庄重得像是在祭坛上安放供品。分工是自然形成的，从不需要协商。外公负责擀皮，因为他的手腕力道最均匀，能把每张皮擀成厚薄一致的正圆形。母亲负责调馅，这是全家公认的最需要判断力的环节——盐放多少、姜切多细、酱油和醋的比例如何平衡——每一个决定都影响着这顿年夜饭的成败。我和表姐负责包，外婆在旁边做质检——她会拿起每一个饺子端详，如果褶子不够均匀或者封口不够紧实，她会不动声色地拆开重包，连批评的话都省了，因为沉默本身就是最严厉的评语。包饺子的过程中有一个不成文的规矩：必须聊天。外婆说「闷头包出来的饺子没有灵魂」。于是桌上的对话从东家长西家短聊到国际局势，从谁家孩子考上了大学聊到菜市场最近涨价了。这些话题本身并不重要，重要的是声音——笑声、争论声、叹息声——它们像调料一样被揉进了饺子馅里。我后来独自在异乡过年的时候也试过自己包饺子。味道是对的，形状也没问题，但总觉得缺了什么。后来我明白了，缺的是那张铺满面粉的大桌子旁边应该坐着的人，以及只有他们才能提供的那种背景噪音——那种嘈杂的、温暖的、属于「家」的声音。
text_pinyin: Zài wǒ de jìyì lǐ, bāo jiǎozi cónglái bú shì pēngrèn, ér shì yī chǎng jiātíng yíshì. Měi nián Chúxī xiàwǔ, wàipó huì zài chúfáng de dà zhuō shàng pū shàng yī céng bǎobǎo de miànfěn, ránhòu bǎ yī dà tuán fā hǎo de miàn fàng zài zhōngyāng——nàge dòngzuò zhuāngzhòng de xiàng shì zài jìtán shàng ānfàng gòngpǐn. Fēngōng shì zìrán xíngchéng de, cóng bù xūyào xiéshāng. Wàigōng fùzé gǎn pí, yīnwèi tā de shǒuwàn lìdào zuì jūnyún, néng bǎ měi zhāng pí gǎn chéng hòubáo yīzhì de zhèng yuánxíng. Mǔqīn fùzé tiáo xiàn, zhè shì quánjiā gōngrèn de zuì xūyào pànduànlì de huánjié——yán fàng duōshao, jiāng qiē duō xì, jiàngyóu hé cù de bǐlì rúhé pínghéng——měi yī gè juédìng dōu yǐngxiǎng zhe zhè dùn niányèfàn de chéngbài. Wǒ hé biǎojiě fùzé bāo, wàipó zài pángbiān zuò zhìjiǎn——tā huì ná qǐ měi yī gè jiǎozi duānxiáng, rúguǒ zhězi bú gòu jūnyún huòzhě fēngkǒu bú gòu jǐnshí, tā huì bú dòng shēngsè de chāi kāi chóng bāo, lián pīpíng de huà dōu shěng le, yīnwèi chénmò běnshēn jiù shì zuì yánlì de píngyǔ. Bāo jiǎozi de guòchéng zhōng yǒu yī gè bù chéng wén de guījǔ: bìxū liáotiān. Wàipó shuō「mēn tóu bāo chūlái de jiǎozi méiyǒu línghún」. Yúshì zhuō shàng de duìhuà cóng dōng jiā cháng xī jiā duǎn liáo dào guójì júshì, cóng shéi jiā háizi kǎo shàng le dàxué liáo dào càishìchǎng zuìjìn zhǎng jià le. Zhèxiē huàtí běnshēn bìng bú zhòngyào, zhòngyào de shì shēngyīn——xiào shēng, zhēnglùn shēng, tànxī shēng——tāmen xiàng tiáoliào yīyàng bèi róu jìn le jiǎozi xiàn lǐ. Wǒ hòulái dúzì zài yìxiāng guò nián de shíhou yě shì guò zìjǐ bāo jiǎozi. Wèidào shì duì de, xíngzhuàng yě méi wèntí, dàn zǒng juéde quē le shénme. Hòulái wǒ míngbai le, quē de shì nà zhāng pū mǎn miànfěn de dà zhuōzi pángbiān yīnggāi zuò zhe de rén, yǐjí zhǐyǒu tāmen cái néng tígōng de nà zhǒng bèijǐng zàoyīn——nà zhǒng cáozá de, wēnnuǎn de, shǔyú「jiā」de shēngyīn.
text_en: In my memory, making dumplings was never cooking—it was a family liturgy. Every New Year's Eve afternoon, Grandmother would dust the big kitchen table with a thin layer of flour, then place a large ball of risen dough at the center—a gesture as solemn as placing an offering on an altar. The division of labor formed naturally, never needing negotiation. Grandfather was in charge of rolling the wrappers, because his wrist pressure was the most even, capable of rolling each wrapper into a perfectly round, uniformly thin circle. Mother was in charge of seasoning the filling, which the whole family recognized as the step requiring the most judgment—how much salt, how finely to mince the ginger, how to balance the ratio of soy sauce and vinegar—every decision affected the success of the New Year's Eve dinner. My cousin and I were in charge of wrapping, with Grandmother doing quality inspection beside us—she would pick up each dumpling and examine it closely. If the pleats weren't even enough or the seal wasn't tight enough, she would quietly unwrap it and re-fold it without a word of criticism, because the silence itself was the sternest review. During dumpling-making there was an unwritten rule: you must chat. Grandmother said 'dumplings wrapped in silence have no soul.' And so the table conversation ranged from neighborhood gossip to international affairs, from whose child got into university to the recent price hikes at the vegetable market. The topics themselves didn't matter; what mattered was the sound—laughter, arguments, sighs—kneaded into the dumpling filling like seasoning. Years later, spending New Year's alone in a distant city, I tried making dumplings by myself. The flavor was right, the shape was fine, but something always felt missing. Eventually I understood: what was missing were the people who should have been sitting around that flour-dusted table, and the background noise only they could provide—that noisy, warm sound that belongs to 'home.'

questions.q_zh: 外婆对不合格的饺子会怎么做？
questions.q_en: What does the grandmother do with substandard dumplings?

options.text: 严厉地批评包饺子的人
options.pinyin: yánlì de pīpíng bāo jiǎozi de rén
options.text_en: Sternly criticize the person who wrapped them

options.text: 不动声色地拆开重包
options.pinyin: bú dòng shēngsè de chāi kāi chóng bāo
options.text_en: Quietly unwrap and re-fold them without a word

options.text: 直接扔掉重新做
options.pinyin: zhíjiē rēng diào chóngxīn zuò
options.text_en: Throw them away and start over

questions.q_zh: 作者后来独自包饺子时觉得缺了什么？
questions.q_en: What did the author feel was missing when making dumplings alone later?

options.text: 外婆的独家调料配方
options.pinyin: wàipó de dújiā tiáoliào pèifāng
options.text_en: Grandmother's secret seasoning recipe

options.text: 家人围坐在一起时嘈杂温暖的声音
options.pinyin: jiārén wéi zuò zài yīqǐ shí cáozá wēnnuǎn de shēngyīn
options.text_en: The noisy, warm sound of family gathered together

options.text: 专业的厨房设备和工具
options.pinyin: zhuānyè de chúfáng shèbèi hé gōngjù
options.text_en: Professional kitchen equipment and tools

title: The Philosophy of a Vegetable Market
text_zh: 我喜欢逛菜市场，不是因为我特别热爱做饭，而是因为菜市场是我见过的最诚实的地方。超市里的蔬菜被包装成完美的样子——颜色统一、大小一致、表面光滑——仿佛它们不是从土地里长出来的，而是从工厂流水线上下来的。但菜市场里的蔬菜保留着泥土、保留着不规则的形状、保留着被虫子咬过的痕迹。这些「缺陷」恰恰证明了它们的真实——它们经历过风雨和阳光，经历过与昆虫的竞争，带着大地的温度来到你面前。菜市场的社交规则也与众不同。这里的交易不是冷冰冰的扫码支付，而是一场充满人情味的对话。卖菜的阿姨会主动告诉你今天哪种菜最新鲜，哪种菜虽然便宜但已经不太好了——这种坦诚在商业世界里几乎是不可想象的。讨价还价也不是对抗，而是一种温柔的拉扯。你说「便宜一点」，她说「已经最低了」，最后她多塞给你两根葱——双方都心满意足，因为交易的本质不是数字的博弈，而是关系的确认。我最喜欢的是菜市场里的声音。叫卖声、砍价声、塑料袋窸窸窣窣的声音、案板上切菜的咚咚声，还有偶尔从某个角落传来的收音机里的评书——这些声音混在一起，形成了一种独特的「菜市场交响曲」。每次我心情低落的时候都会去菜市场走一圈。看到那些堆得满满当当的新鲜蔬果，听到那些充满烟火气的吆喝声，我就觉得——这个世界还在好好运转着，人们还在认真地为下一顿饭做准备。这件事本身就是一种安慰。
text_pinyin: Wǒ xǐhuan guàng càishìchǎng, bú shì yīnwèi wǒ tèbié rè'ài zuòfàn, ér shì yīnwèi càishìchǎng shì wǒ jiàn guò de zuì chéngshí de dìfang. Chāoshì lǐ de shūcài bèi bāozhuāng chéng wánměi de yàngzi——yánsè tǒngyī, dàxiǎo yīzhì, biǎomiàn guānghuá——fǎngfú tāmen bú shì cóng tǔdì lǐ zhǎng chūlái de, ér shì cóng gōngchǎng liúshuǐxiàn shàng xiàlái de. Dàn càishìchǎng lǐ de shūcài bǎoliú zhe nítǔ, bǎoliú zhe bù guīzé de xíngzhuàng, bǎoliú zhe bèi chóngzi yǎo guò de hénjì. Zhèxiē「quēxiàn」qiàqià zhèngmíng le tāmen de zhēnshí——tāmen jīnglì guò fēngyǔ hé yángguāng, jīnglì guò yǔ kūnchóng de jìngzhēng, dài zhe dàdì de wēndù lái dào nǐ miànqián. Càishìchǎng de shèjiāo guīzé yě yǔzhòngbùtóng. Zhèlǐ de jiāoyì bú shì lěngbīngbīng de sǎo mǎ zhīfù, ér shì yī chǎng chōngmǎn rénqíngwèi de duìhuà. Mài cài de āyí huì zhǔdòng gàosù nǐ jīntiān nǎ zhǒng cài zuì xīnxiān, nǎ zhǒng cài suīrán piányi dàn yǐjīng bú tài hǎo le——zhè zhǒng tǎnchéng zài shāngyè shìjiè lǐ jīhū shì bù kě xiǎngxiàng de. Tǎojià huánjià yě bú shì duìkàng, ér shì yī zhǒng wēnróu de lāchě. Nǐ shuō「piányi yīdiǎn」, tā shuō「yǐjīng zuìdī le」, zuìhòu tā duō sāi gěi nǐ liǎng gēn cōng——shuāngfāng dōu xīnmǎnyìzú, yīnwèi jiāoyì de běnzhì bú shì shùzì de bóyì, ér shì guānxi de quèrèn. Wǒ zuì xǐhuan de shì càishìchǎng lǐ de shēngyīn. Jiàomài shēng, kǎnjià shēng, sùliào dài xīxīsūsū de shēngyīn, ànbǎn shàng qiē cài de dōngdōng shēng, hái yǒu ǒu'ěr cóng mǒu gè jiǎoluò chuán lái de shōuyīnjī lǐ de pínshū——zhèxiē shēngyīn hùn zài yīqǐ, xíngchéng le yī zhǒng dútè de「càishìchǎng jiāoxiǎngqǔ」. Měi cì wǒ xīnqíng dīluò de shíhou dōu huì qù càishìchǎng zǒu yī quān. Kàn dào nàxiē duī de mǎnmǎndāngdāng de xīnxiān shūguǒ, tīng dào nàxiē chōngmǎn yānhuǒ qì de yāohe shēng, wǒ jiù juéde——zhège shìjiè hái zài hǎohǎo yùnzhuǎn zhe, rénmen hái zài rènzhēn de wèi xià yī dùn fàn zuò zhǔnbèi. Zhè jiàn shì běnshēn jiù shì yī zhǒng ānwèi.
text_en: I like strolling through vegetable markets, not because I'm particularly passionate about cooking, but because the vegetable market is the most honest place I've ever seen. Supermarket vegetables are packaged into perfect forms—uniform color, consistent size, smooth surfaces—as if they weren't grown from the earth but came off a factory assembly line. But vegetables at the market retain their dirt, their irregular shapes, their traces of insect bites. These 'imperfections' are precisely what prove their authenticity—they've weathered rain and sunshine, competed with insects, and arrived before you carrying the temperature of the earth. The social rules of the vegetable market are also distinctive. Transactions here aren't cold QR-code payments but conversations full of human warmth. The auntie selling vegetables will proactively tell you which variety is freshest today, and which, though cheap, has already gone off—a candor nearly unimaginable in the commercial world. Haggling isn't confrontation but a gentle tug-of-war. You say 'a little cheaper,' she says 'it's already the lowest,' and in the end she stuffs two extra scallions into your bag—both sides satisfied, because the essence of the transaction isn't a numerical game but a confirmation of relationship. What I love most is the sound of the vegetable market. Hawking calls, bargaining voices, the rustling of plastic bags, the thud-thud of chopping on cutting boards, and occasionally from some corner, a storytelling program drifting from a radio—these sounds blend together into a unique 'vegetable market symphony.' Whenever I'm feeling low, I take a walk through the market. Seeing those heaping piles of fresh fruits and vegetables, hearing those calls full of life's earthly warmth, I feel—this world is still running along just fine, people are still earnestly preparing for the next meal. That fact in itself is a kind of comfort.

questions.q_zh: 作者认为菜市场蔬菜的「缺陷」说明了什么？

questions.q_zh: 作者心情低落时为什么会去菜市场？
questions.q_en: Why does the author go to the vegetable market when feeling low?

options.text: 可以买到便宜的新鲜蔬菜

options.text: 看到人们认真为下一顿饭做准备让人感到安慰
options.pinyin: kàn dào rénmen rènzhēn wèi xià yī dùn fàn zuò zhǔnbèi ràng rén gǎndào ānwèi
options.text_en: Seeing people earnestly preparing for the next meal is comforting

title: The Tenderness of Congee
text_zh: 中国人对粥有一种特殊的情感依赖。它不是正餐，不够庄重；它也不是零食，不够随意。它存在于一个微妙的中间地带——它是病中的陪伴、清晨的过渡、深夜的抚慰、以及所有需要温柔的时刻的默认选择。我外婆煮粥有一套不成文的法则。首先，水和米的比例要根据季节调整——冬天的粥要浓一些，因为人需要更多的热量和饱腹感；夏天的粥要稀一些，配上一碟凉拌黄瓜，清爽解暑。其次，煮粥必须用小火慢熬，中途不能加水。她说「好粥是等出来的，不是催出来的」，这句话的适用范围远远超出了厨房。最重要的是，粥煮好以后不能立刻盛出来，要在锅里「醒」一会儿——让米粒充分吸收水分，释放出淀粉的黏稠感，形成那层润泽的「粥油」。外婆说那层粥油是「整锅粥的精华」，应该留给家里最需要滋补的人。在我家的情感词典里，「给你煮碗粥」是一句比「我爱你」更具体的表达。当你发烧躺在床上，母亲端来的那碗白粥——清淡、温热、容易消化——它不试图治愈你的疾病，但它承诺陪你度过最虚弱的时刻。当你失恋深夜回家，桌上温着的那锅小米粥——不问你发生了什么，不试图给你人生建议，只是安静地等你喝下去。粥的智慧就在于它从不试图成为主角。它是一种彻底谦逊的食物，甘愿做配角，甘愿做背景，甘愿在所有人都忽略它的时候，安静地提供那份不张扬的温暖。也许这就是为什么，在所有的中国食物里，粥是最像母爱的那一种。
text_pinyin: Zhōngguó rén duì zhōu yǒu yī zhǒng tèshū de qínggǎn yīlài. Tā bú shì zhèngcān, bú gòu zhuāngzhòng; tā yě bú shì língshí, bú gòu suíyì. Tā cúnzài yú yī gè wēimiào de zhōngjiān dìdài——tā shì bìng zhōng de péibàn, qīngchén de guòdù, shēnyè de fǔwèi, yǐjí suǒyǒu xūyào wēnróu de shíkè de mòrèn xuǎnzé. Wǒ wàipó zhǔ zhōu yǒu yī tào bù chéng wén de fǎzé. Shǒuxiān, shuǐ hé mǐ de bǐlì yào gēnjù jìjié tiáozhěng——dōngtiān de zhōu yào nóng yīxiē, yīnwèi rén xūyào gèng duō de rèliàng hé bǎofùgǎn; xiàtiān de zhōu yào xī yīxiē, pèi shàng yī dié liángbàn huángguā, qīngshuǎng jiě shǔ. Qícì, zhǔ zhōu bìxū yòng xiǎo huǒ màn áo, zhōngtú bù néng jiā shuǐ. Tā shuō「hǎo zhōu shì děng chūlái de, bú shì cuī chūlái de」, zhè jù huà de shìyòng fànwéi yuǎnyuǎn chāochū le chúfáng. Zuì zhòngyào de shì, zhōu zhǔ hǎo yǐhòu bù néng lìkè chéng chūlái, yào zài guō lǐ「xǐng」yīhuìr——ràng mǐ lì chōngfèn xīshōu shuǐfèn, shìfàng chū diànfěn de niánchóu gǎn, xíngchéng nà céng rùnzé de「zhōu yóu」. Wàipó shuō nà céng zhōu yóu shì「zhěng guō zhōu de jīnghuá」, yīnggāi liú gěi jiā lǐ zuì xūyào zībǔ de rén. Zài wǒ jiā de qínggǎn cídiǎn lǐ,「gěi nǐ zhǔ wǎn zhōu」shì yī jù bǐ「wǒ ài nǐ」gèng jùtǐ de biǎodá. Dāng nǐ fāshāo tǎng zài chuáng shàng, mǔqīn duān lái de nà wǎn bái zhōu——qīngdàn, wēnrè, róngyì xiāohuà——tā bú shìtú zhìyù nǐ de jíbìng, dàn tā chéngnuò péi nǐ dùguò zuì xūruò de shíkè. Dāng nǐ shīliàn shēnyè huí jiā, zhuō shàng wēn zhe de nà guō xiǎomǐ zhōu——bú wèn nǐ fāshēng le shénme, bú shìtú gěi nǐ rénshēng jiànyì, zhǐ shì ānjìng de děng nǐ hē xiàqù. Zhōu de zhìhuì jiù zàiyú tā cóng bú shìtú chéngwéi zhǔjué. Tā shì yī zhǒng chèdǐ qiānxùn de shíwù, gānyuàn zuò pèijué, gānyuàn zuò bèijǐng, gānyuàn zài suǒyǒu rén dōu hūlüè tā de shíhou, ānjìng de tígōng nà fèn bù zhāngyáng de wēnnuǎn. Yěxǔ zhè jiù shì wèishéme, zài suǒyǒu de Zhōngguó shíwù lǐ, zhōu shì zuì xiàng mǔ'ài de nà yī zhǒng.
text_en: Chinese people have a special emotional dependence on congee. It isn't a proper meal—not dignified enough; it isn't a snack—not casual enough. It exists in a subtle middle ground—it is companionship during illness, a transition at dawn, solace late at night, and the default choice for every moment requiring tenderness. My grandmother had an unwritten code for cooking congee. First, the ratio of water to rice must adjust by season—winter congee should be thicker, because people need more warmth and satiety; summer congee should be thinner, served with a small dish of cold cucumber salad, refreshing and heat-relieving. Second, congee must be simmered over low heat, and no water may be added midway. She said 'good congee is waited into being, not rushed'—a maxim whose applicability extends far beyond the kitchen. Most importantly, once the congee is done it shouldn't be served immediately but must 'rest' in the pot for a while—letting the rice grains fully absorb moisture, release the starch's viscosity, and form that lustrous layer of 'congee cream.' Grandmother said that layer of congee cream is 'the essence of the whole pot' and should be saved for whoever in the family most needs nourishment. In my family's emotional dictionary, 'let me make you a bowl of congee' is a more concrete expression than 'I love you.' When you're lying in bed with a fever, the bowl of plain congee mother brings—light, warm, easy to digest—doesn't attempt to cure your illness, but promises to accompany you through your weakest moment. When you come home late at night after a heartbreak, the pot of millet congee kept warm on the table—asks nothing about what happened, offers no life advice, simply waits quietly for you to drink it. The wisdom of congee lies in the fact that it never tries to be the protagonist. It is a thoroughly humble food, content to play a supporting role, content to be the background, content to quietly provide its understated warmth while everyone overlooks it. Perhaps that is why, among all Chinese foods, congee is the one most like a mother's love.

questions.q_zh: 外婆说「好粥是等出来的，不是催出来的」，这句话暗示了什么更广泛的道理？
questions.q_en: What broader truth does the grandmother's saying 'good congee is waited into being, not rushed' imply?

options.text: 因为粥是母亲最擅长做的食物

title: The Farewell Banquet That Wasn't About Food
text_zh: 我出国前的最后一顿饭是在老家的一个小饭馆里吃的。那家饭馆没有精致的装修，没有优雅的背景音乐，甚至连菜单都是手写在墙上的白板上的。但它是父亲从小吃到大的地方，也是我们家每逢重要时刻都会去的地方——无论是庆祝还是告别。那天点的菜我至今记得清清楚楚：一盘糖醋排骨、一碗酸辣汤、一份干煸四季豆、一盘红烧鱼、一碟花生米。这些都是我从小最爱吃的菜，但那天每一道菜的味道都不太一样——不是厨师变了，而是我的味觉被即将到来的离别浸染了某种苦涩。父亲那天话很少。他平时就不爱说话，但那天的沉默有一种不同的质地——像是有很多话堵在喉咙里，但他找不到合适的出口。他只是不停地给我夹菜，一筷子又一筷子，好像要把未来几年我吃不到的家乡味道都提前储存在我的胃里。母亲则相反，她说了很多话，但仔细听会发现那些话都在绕圈子——「那边冬天冷不冷」「记得按时吃饭」「衣服带够了没有」——这些琐碎的叮嘱是她表达爱的唯一方式，因为「我舍不得你走」这句话对她来说太重了，重到她说不出口。最后结账的时候，饭馆老板坚持不收钱。他拍了拍父亲的肩膀说：「孩子出远门，这顿算我的。」父亲没有推辞，只是点了点头。我从没见过父亲那么轻易地接受别人的好意——后来我才明白，那一刻他不是不想推辞，而是他怕一开口就会哭出来。回去的路上，我坐在后座看着窗外掠过的街景，突然意识到：那顿饭的每一道菜都不是食物，而是一句没有说出口的话。糖醋排骨是「祝你甜蜜」，酸辣汤是「人生有酸有辣」，干煸四季豆是「日子要过得干脆利落」，红烧鱼是「年年有余」，而那碟花生米——是「平平安安」。
text_pinyin: Wǒ chūguó qián de zuìhòu yī dùn fàn shì zài lǎojiā de yī gè xiǎo fànguǎn lǐ chī de. Nà jiā fànguǎn méiyǒu jīngzhì de zhuāngxiū, méiyǒu yōuyǎ de bèijǐng yīnyuè, shènzhì lián càidān dōu shì shǒuxiě zài qiáng shàng de báibǎn shàng de. Dàn tā shì fùqīn cóng xiǎo chī dào dà de dìfang, yě shì wǒmen jiā měi féng zhòngyào shíkè dōu huì qù de dìfang——wúlùn shì qìngzhù háishi gàobié. Nà tiān diǎn de cài wǒ zhìjīn jìde qīngqīngchǔchǔ: yī pán tángcù páigǔ, yī wǎn suānlà tāng, yī fèn gānbiān sìjìdòu, yī pán hóngshāo yú, yī dié huāshēngmǐ. Zhèxiē dōu shì wǒ cóng xiǎo zuì ài chī de cài, dàn nà tiān měi yī dào cài de wèidào dōu bú tài yīyàng——bú shì chúshī biàn le, ér shì wǒ de wèijué bèi jíjiāng dàolái de líbié jìnrǎn le mǒu zhǒng kǔsè. Fùqīn nà tiān huà hěn shǎo. Tā píngshí jiù bú ài shuōhuà, dàn nà tiān de chénmò yǒu yī zhǒng bùtóng de zhìdì——xiàng shì yǒu hěn duō huà dǔ zài hóulóng lǐ, dàn tā zhǎo bú dào héshì de chūkǒu. Tā zhǐ shì bù tíng de gěi wǒ jiā cài, yī kuàizi yòu yī kuàizi, hǎoxiàng yào bǎ wèilái jǐ nián wǒ chī bú dào de jiāxiāng wèidào dōu tíqián chúcún zài wǒ de wèi lǐ. Mǔqīn zé xiāngfǎn, tā shuō le hěn duō huà, dàn zǐxì tīng huì fāxiàn nàxiē huà dōu zài rào quānzi——「nàbiān dōngtiān lěng bù lěng」「jìde ànshí chīfàn」「yīfu dài gòu le méiyǒu」——zhèxiē suǒsuì de dīngzhǔ shì tā biǎodá ài de wéiyī fāngshì, yīnwèi「wǒ shěbude nǐ zǒu」zhè jù huà duì tā lái shuō tài zhòng le, zhòng dào tā shuō bù chūkǒu. Zuìhòu jiézhàng de shíhou, fànguǎn lǎobǎn jiānchí bù shōu qián. Tā pāi le pāi fùqīn de jiānbǎng shuō:「Háizi chū yuǎnmén, zhè dùn suàn wǒ de.」Fùqīn méiyǒu tuīcí, zhǐ shì diǎn le diǎn tóu. Wǒ cóng méi jiàn guò fùqīn nàme qīngyì de jiēshòu biérén de hǎoyì——hòulái wǒ cái míngbai, nà yī kè tā bú shì bù xiǎng tuīcí, ér shì tā pà yī kāikǒu jiù huì kū chūlái. Huíqù de lù shàng, wǒ zuò zài hòuzuò kàn zhe chuāng wài lüè guò de jiējǐng, tūrán yìshí dào: nà dùn fàn de měi yī dào cài dōu bú shì shíwù, ér shì yī jù méiyǒu shuō chūkǒu de huà. Tángcù páigǔ shì「zhù nǐ tiánmì」, suānlà tāng shì「rénshēng yǒu suān yǒu là」, gānbiān sìjìdòu shì「rìzi yào guò de gāncuì lìluo」, hóngshāo yú shì「niánnián yǒu yú」, ér nà dié huāshēngmǐ——shì「píngpíng ān'ān」.
text_en: My last meal before going abroad was eaten at a small restaurant in my hometown. It had no refined decor, no elegant background music; even the menu was handwritten on a whiteboard on the wall. But it was the place my father had eaten at since childhood, and the place our family went for every important occasion—whether celebration or farewell. I still remember clearly what we ordered that day: a plate of sweet-and-sour ribs, a bowl of hot-and-sour soup, a serving of dry-fried green beans, a plate of braised fish, and a small dish of peanuts. These were all my favorite foods since childhood, but every dish tasted slightly different that day—not because the chef had changed, but because my taste buds had been steeped in a certain bitterness by the approaching farewell. Father spoke very little that day. He was never talkative, but that day's silence had a different texture—as if many words were stuck in his throat, but he couldn't find a proper exit for them. He just kept serving me food, chopstick after chopstick, as though trying to pre-store in my stomach all the hometown flavors I wouldn't taste for years to come. Mother was the opposite—she talked a great deal, but if you listened carefully you'd notice all her words circled around the same things: 'Is winter cold over there?' 'Remember to eat on time.' 'Did you pack enough clothes?'—these trivial reminders were her only way of expressing love, because 'I can't bear to see you go' was too heavy a sentence for her, so heavy she couldn't say it aloud. When it came time to pay, the restaurant owner insisted on refusing payment. He patted father's shoulder and said, 'The child's heading far from home—this meal's on me.' Father didn't decline; he just nodded. I'd never seen father accept someone's kindness so easily—only later did I understand that in that moment, it wasn't that he didn't want to refuse, but that he was afraid if he opened his mouth he'd cry. On the drive back, sitting in the back seat watching the streetscape flash past the window, I suddenly realized: every dish in that meal wasn't food but an unspoken sentence. The sweet-and-sour ribs meant 'wishing you sweetness,' the hot-and-sour soup meant 'life has its sour and its spicy,' the dry-fried green beans meant 'live your days with clean decisiveness,' the braised fish meant 'abundance year after year,' and that small dish of peanuts—meant 'safe and sound.'

questions.q_zh: 父亲那天为什么没有拒绝饭馆老板的好意？
questions.q_en: Why didn't the father refuse the restaurant owner's kindness that day?

options.text: 因为他觉得老板欠他人情

options.text_en: Because he felt the owner owed him a favor

options.text: 因为他怕一开口就会哭出来

options.text_en: Because he was afraid he'd cry if he opened his mouth

questions.q_zh: 作者最终意识到那顿饭的每道菜代表了什么？
questions.q_en: What did the author ultimately realize each dish in the meal represented?

options.text_en: A nutritional combination carefully chosen by the parents

options.text_en: An unspoken blessing or wish

options.text: 饭馆老板推荐的招牌菜
